content.problemStatement:
The Government of Siruseri is no different from any other when it comes to being "capital-centric" in its policies. Recently the government decided to set up a nationwide fiber-optic network to take Siruseri into the digital age. And as usual, this decision was implemented in a capital centric manner --- from each city in the country, a fiber optic cable was laid to the capital! Thus, traffic between any two cities had to go through the capital.

Soon, it became apparent that this was not quite a clever idea, since any breakdown at the capital resulted in the disconnection of services between other cities. So, in the second phase, the government plans to connect a few more pairs of cities directly by fiber-optic cables. The government has specified that this is to be done in such a way that the disruption of services at any one city will still leave the rest of the country connected.

The government has data on the cost of laying fiber optic cables between every pair of cities. You task is to compute the minimum cost of additional cabling required to ensure the requirement described above is met.

For example, if Siruseri has 444 cities numbered 1,2,31,2,31,2,3 and 444 where 111 is the capital and further suppose that the cost of laying cables between these cities are as given in the table below:



Note that the government has already connected the capital with every other city. So, if we connect the cities 222 and 333 as well as 333 and 444, you can check that disruption of service at any one city will still leave the other cities connected. The cost of connecting these two pairs is 4+6=104 + 6 = 104+6=10. The same effect could have been achieved by connecting 222 and 333 as well as 222 and 444, which would have cost 4+5=94 + 5 = 94+5=9. You can check that this is the best you can do.

Your task is to write a program that allows the government to determine the minimum cost it has to incur in laying additional cables to fulfil the requirement.

###Input:


The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN indicating the number of cities in Siruseri. You may assume that the capital city is always numbered 111.
This is followed by NNN lines of input each containing NNN integers.
The jthj^{th}jth integer on line iii is the cost of connecting city iii with city jjj. The jthj^{th}jth integer on line iii will be the same as the ithi^{th}ith integer on line jjj (since the links are bidirectional) and the ithi^{th}ith entry on line iii will always be 000 (there is no cost in connecting a city with itself).
###Output:

A single integer indicating the minimum total cost of the links to be added to ensure that disruption of services at one city does not disconnect the rest of the cities.

###Constraints:


1≤N≤20001 \leq N \leq 20001≤N≤2000.
0≤0 \leq0≤ costs given in the input ≤100000\leq 100000≤100000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N indicating the number of cities in Siruseri. This is followed by N lines of input each containing N integers. The jth integer on line i is the cost of connecting city i with city j.
content.outputFormat: A single integer indicating the minimum total cost of the links to be added to ensure that disruption of services at one city does not disconnect the rest of the cities.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The minimum cost is achieved by adding edges between cities 2 and 3, and between cities 3 and 4, with a total cost of 4 + 5 = 9.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) algorithm with a Union-Find data structure. The goal is to find the minimum cost of adding edges to the graph such that the removal of any node does not disconnect the graph. We start by initializing the Union-Find data structure and then iterate over all possible edges in non-decreasing order of their costs. For each edge, we check if adding it to the MST would create a cycle. If not, we add it to the MST and update the Union-Find data structure. We continue this process until we have added N-2 edges to the MST (since we already have N-1 edges connecting each city to the capital). The total cost of the edges in the MST is the minimum cost required to ensure the graph remains connected even if one node is removed.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2 log N) — due to sorting the edges and using the Union-Find data structure
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — for storing the Union-Find data structure and the MST
